sections.heading: A guide to irrigation requirement of wheat crop in FAO-developed agro-climatic zones of Punjab, Pakistan
sections.text: Research Open access Published: 27 March 2025 Volume 3 , article number 41 ( 2025 ) Cite this article You have full access to this open access article Download PDF Save article View saved research

sections.heading: Article summary
sections.text: AI generated

sections.heading: Abstract
sections.text: Water scarcity is a global concern exacerbated by human activities, including water mismanagement. The water shortage intensely impacts food production, as inadequate water is applied to the field for irrigation. Proper irrigation systems help to boost crop productivity and ensure food security. In arid and semi-arid regions, a limited amount of rainfall demands an understanding of crop water requirements (CWR). Climate change is the major suspect that further complicates irrigation management because as the temperature rises, evapotranspiration increases, leading to greater water demand by crops. Changes in rainfall patterns contribute to drought or floods that adversely affect crop production. To address these challenges, irrigation scheduling is essential to mitigate the effect of climate change on crop yield. This study determines the CWR and irrigation scheduling of Wheat in six districts of Punjab across the agro-climatic zones defined by the Food and Agricultural Organization (FAO), using CROPWAT software. Evapotranspiration was calculated using the Penman–Monteith method that utilize the climate data from 2013 to 2023. The selected station includes Mandibahauddin (MBD), Faisalabad (FSD), Toba Tek Singh (TTS), Bahawalpur (BWP), Bahawalnagar (BWN), and Khanpur (KNP). The results indicate that CWR varies in different stages of the crop; the lowest water requirement is in the initial stage, and the highest water requirement is in the mid-stage during crop development. During the dry season, the water requirement is high because rain can’t fulfill the crop’s water needs. Notably, southern Punjab BWP and KNP show maximum irrigation water requirements of 179, and 184.2 mm due to the dry climate compared to central region MBD, highlighting the influence of climatic variations. Unlike the previous studies focusing on specific districts, this research paper provides comprehensive irrigation scheduling across Punjab’s agro-climatic zones. Future studies should expand to include other crops to refine irrigation practices further. Graphical abstract

sections.heading: 1 Introduction
sections.text: The scarcity of water and the degradation of water quality are being globally intensified by climate change. In Pakistan, due to significant susceptibility to climate change, agriculture frequently experiences negative consequences such as severe storms, floods, and droughts. [ 1 ]. Pakistan ranks 5th globally in the frequency of extreme weather events as per the Global Climate Risk Index [ 2 ]. In Pakistan, natural resources, particularly water, soil, and plants, are key for the survival of animals and humans but are continuously affected by climate change. Water is considered the quintessential substance among all elements, unparalleled in its remarkable properties, and is a crucial part of human well-being and socio-economic development. Water is the most important substance on Earth with approximately 0.3% of the global water resources available [ 3 , 4 ]. Surface water supplies are also shrinking due to industrialization and burdening the groundwater resources, and approximately 60 billion cubic meters (Bm3) of groundwater are annually pumped out [ 5 , 6 ]. When considering the water requirement in Pakistan, the agricultural sector is one of the most dominant water consumers and utilizes 70% of the world’s accessible water, with approximately 60% squandered [ 7 , 8 ]. In the area of 796,096 square kilometers [ 9 ], the arid regions make up two-thirds of Pakistan’s land area and have shallow water tables, the elevated air temperatures may cause faster evaporation, which would increase the aridity of the soil [ 10 ]. The nation’s problems with water scarcity could worsen if evapotranspiration rates keep rising and reservoirs start depleting quickly. Pakistan lies in an arid climate and relies heavily on irrigation due to insufficient precipitation on its agricultural lands and irrigation supports approximately, 18 million hectares of cultivable land [ 11 ]. Pakistan’s irrigation system ranks among the world’s largest sources of irrigation [ 12 ]. The total water resources in Pakistan is 180 bcm (billion cubic meters) from the Indus River system whereas, rainfall is the second, and groundwater is the third source with annual contributions of approximately 50 bcm and 50–60 bcm respectively [ 13 ]. In Pakistan, surface water is vital for agricultural activities. Approximately 95% of freshwater is used for agriculture and only 5% is allocated for domestic use [ 14 ]. Moreover, agriculture constitutes the primary source of income for 72% of Pakistan’s population. Crops including Wheat, Sugarcane, rice, cotton, and bananas are essential for the nation’s sustainable food demands and significantly boost its economic growth. Wheat the most important staple food of Pakistan plays a crucial role in the country’s agriculture. Punjab soil is favorable for wheat production as its cultivation surpassed 500,000 acres in the province in 2023. Pakistan’s economy hinges primarily on agriculture, with 70% of its population depending on this sector. Shifts in rainfall patterns and rising temperatures directly affect water resources and agriculture. Each year, escalating temperatures intensify the need for crop irrigation due to heightened evapotranspiration rates, demanding more water to sustain crop vitality [ 15 ]. So, reducing consumption and improving resource management is crucial. Punjab has the largest area of irrigation 97% where maximum irrigation is provided to wheat, which is 60% [ 16 ] which contributes to 75% of the region's wheat production [ 9 ]. The state of Punjab in Pakistan is facing water scarcity due to the fast depletion of groundwater extracted for agricultural needs, especially in central Punjab because of the adverse effects of climate change and inefficient water use [ 17 ]. Punjab is a primary consumer of water for irrigation, so the irrigation system needs to be managed properly by assessing the water requirement system. To meet the irrigation demands, it is necessary to understand the CWR and the irrigation scheduli

sections.heading: 2 Materials and methods
sections.text: 2.1 Software CROPWAT is a window-based model developed by the FAO for calculating evapotranspiration and optimizing irrigation scheduling and design. This application uses the FAO’s Penman–Monteith Equation to determine evapotranspiration [ 12 ]. The key factors influencing the potential evapotranspiration value include temperature, wind speed, solar radiation, ambient temperature, and air humidity in the Monteith-Penman equation. With CROPWAT 8.0 software, we can determine the crop’s water requirements throughout the growing season and manage irrigation using rainwater effectively in the field [ 27 ]. The current scheduling techniques employed by farmers are outdated and ineffective. It is time to modernize these practices by utilizing CROPWAT to asses CWR, organize relevant data, model irrigation needs based on climate, crop, and soil information, and implement efficient scheduling [ 8 ]. The crop coefficient performs the irrigation schedule as it allocates the required amount of water to the crop. However, metrological data such as wind speed, temperature, and harvesting date greatly affect the Kc value. Therefore, it is important to make corrections to the data. 2.2 Study area Punjab is the second-largest and most populated province, accounting for 25.9% of the country’s total land area, which spans 20.63 million hectares, out of this 72% is suitable for cropping [ 28 ]. Punjab’s total cropped area accounts for 16.68 million hectares. In 2023–24, the net sown area of wheat covered 9.6 million hectares, accounting for 53% of the total [ 29 ], giving 28,175.5 tons of wheat production [ 30 ]. This study was conducted in the seven Agro-climatic zones of Punjab classified by FAO based on the Thornthwaite Moisture Index (TMI). MBD FSD, TTS, BWN, BWP, and KNP are the representatives of each zone, as shown in Fig. 1 . None of the stations was selected from Zone 1 because this is a rain-fed zone as wheat in this zone does not require irrigation and the water requirement of the crop is fulfilled by rainwater. The complete description of the area, climate, and annual rainfall of these districts is mentioned in Table 1 . Fig. 1 Full size image The figure shows the selected districts from each Agro-climatic zone of Punjab that were used for the crop water requirement and irrigation scheduling of wheat Table 1 Key characteristics of the study area, highlighting geographical coordinates, elevation, climate type, and annual rainfall Full size table 2.3 Data CROPWAT model required mainly three types of data; climatic, soil, and crop data. In the current study, we used the climatic data obtained from the Pakistan Metrological Department (PMD). Climatic data includes five parameters; minimum and maximum temperature, rainfall, sunshine hour, wind speed, relative humidity, and physical data such as latitude, longitude, and altitude. These datasets were utilized to calculate the CWR. The lowest minimum average temperature observed at the selected station was recorded in January and the highest maximum average temperature was recorded in May and June, standard deviation is applied to see the data consistency as shown in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2 Full size image Monthly mean of Minimum and Maximum temperature of the selected station from 2013 to 2023 a Mandibahauddin, b Faisalabad, c T.T Singh, d Bahawalnagar, e Bahawalpur, f Khanpur The precipitation pattern of the study area shows maximum rainfall in the monsoon season from July to September whereas, minimum rain was recorded from December to February under the influence of western disturbance is exhibited in Fig. 3 . The parameters of humidity, wind speed, and sunshine hours were also considered as they are the major factors for evapotranspiration. January is the most humid month in the study area. The maximum sunshine hours from the selected six stations are observed in May. The data of average humidity, sunshine hours, and wind speed of the study area are displayed in Fig. 4 . sections.text: CROPWAT is a window-based model developed by the FAO for calculating evapotranspiration and optimizing irrigation scheduling and design. With CROPWAT 8.0 software, we can determine the crop’s water requirements throughout the growing season and manage irrigation using rainwater effectively in the field [ 27 ]. The current scheduling techniques employed by farmers are outdated and ineffective. It is time to modernize these practices by utilizing CROPWAT to asses CWR, organize relevant data, model irrigation needs based on climate, crop, and soil information, and implement efficient scheduling [ 8 ]. However, metrological data such as wind speed, temperature, and harvesting date greatly affect the Kc value. Therefore, it is important to make corrections to the data.

sections.heading: 2.3 Data
sections.text: CROPWAT model required mainly three types of data; climatic, soil, and crop data. In the current study, we used the climatic data obtained from the Pakistan Metrological Department (PMD). Climatic data includes five parameters; minimum and maximum temperature, rainfall, sunshine hour, wind speed, relative humidity, and physical data such as latitude, longitude, and altitude. These datasets were utilized to calculate the CWR. The lowest minimum average temperature observed at the selected station was recorded in January and the highest maximum average temperature was recorded in May and June, standard deviation is applied to see the data consistency as shown in Fig. 2 . sections.heading: 2.4 Crop data
sections.text: Wheat is the major crop of Punjab and occupies the largest portion of cropped land. In 2015, the wheat crop was planted in 42% of the total crop area [ 37 ]. To compute the Crop Irrigation Requirement, CROPWAT requires several crop parameters, including planting and harvesting dates, number of days at different stages of growth, rooting depth, allowable depletion, crop coefficient (Kc), and yield response. The date of planting and harvesting of crops is selected after a thorough review of different websites and sources, and the remaining factors were taken from FAO software CROPWAT.

sections.heading: 2.5 Soil data
sections.text: The soil type of the study area ranges from sandy loam to loam. The CROPWAT software requires fundamental soil data, including maximum rainfall infiltration rate, total soil moisture available, maximum root depth, Soil moisture depletion, and initial soil moisture content. The software used for the current study includes information on soil data. The required soil type and data are imported from the software.

sections.heading: 2.6 Reference evapotranspiration and effective rainfall
sections.text: Evapotranspiration refers to the combined loss of water through transpiration from plants and evaporation from the soil surface. The FAO Penman–Monteith equation is used for the calculation of evapotranspiration by the CROPWAT model as shown in Eq. 1 [ 19 ]. $$ET=\frac{\Delta \left(Rn-G\right)+PaCp\frac{(es-ea)}{ra}}{\Delta +\gamma (1+\frac{rs}{rs})}$$ (1) ∆ = Slope of vapor pressure curve. Rn = Net radiation. G = Soil heat flux density. Pa = average air density at constant pressure. Cp = specific heat of air. es—ea = vapor pressure deficiency of the soil. γ = Psychometric constant. rs = surface resistance. ra = Aerodynamic resistance. ETo is reference evapotranspiration measured in millimeters per day. ETo can be represented by the following Eq. 2 $$ETo=\frac{0.408\Delta \left(Rn-G\right)+\gamma \frac{900}{T+273}u2(es-ea)}{\Delta +\gamma (1+0.34u2)}$$ (2) where es and ea = saturation. T = average temperature at 2-m height. u2 = the speed of wind at a height of 2 m (ms −1 ). Effective rainfall is the portion of rainfall that is retained in the soil profile and contributes to crop growth. The effective rainfall in the CROPWAT model was calculated using the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Soil Conservation Service (SCS) formula shown in Eqs. 3 and 4 [ 38 ]. $${P}_{eff}=\frac{{P}_{total}(125-0.26{P}_{total})}{125}\; for\; {P}_{total}\le \left(\frac{250}{3}\right)mm\;(per\; month)$$ (3) $${P}_{eff}=125+0.1{P}_{total}\; for\; {P}_{total} >250mm \left(per\; month\right)$$ (4) \({P}_{eff}=\) Effective rainfall (mm). \({P}_{total}=\) Total rainfall (mm).

sections.heading: 2.7 Crop water requirement
sections.text: CWR is defined as the quantity of water needed to meet evapotranspiration demands for optimal crop growth, and it is calculated by using Eq. 5 [ 39 ] $${ET}_{crop}={ET}_{O}{\times K}_{c}$$ (5)

sections.heading: 2.8 Irrigation water requirement
sections.text: The irrigation water requirement is the quantity of water required to meet CWR after accounting for any effective rainfall. This irrigation water requirement is for disease-free crops growing in extensive fields with non-restrictive water and soil conditions and sufficient fertility. The irrigation water requirement is greater than the CWR when agriculture relies solely on irrigation. However, when both irrigation and rainfall are used, the irrigation water requirement is slightly lower than the CWR due to the supplementary water provided by rainfall. It can be calculated using the Eq. 6 . The net irrigation requirement of a crop is calculated by finding the difference between effective rain and crop evapotranspiration as given in Eq. ( 7 ) [ 40 ]. $$IWR=(CWR-{P}_{eff})$$ (6) $$\text{NIR}=\text{ETc}-\text{Eff}.\text{rain}$$ (7)

sections.heading: 3 Results and discussion
sections.text: 3.1 Reference evapotranspiration The information provided to the CROPWAT for the selected study area encompassed details such as the crop variety, climatic station, soil type, and planting date. By entering these details into the software, it forecasts the evapotranspiration, total irrigation needs, and effective rainfall for the wheat crop. The reference evapotranspiration of wheat crops in the selected stations is shown in Fig. 5 . The results illustrate that evapotranspiration was highest in June and lowest in January. If the evapotranspiration value is high, it indicates increased evaporation, leading to a higher CWR. In contrast, if evapotranspiration is low, the crop’s water needs will be reduced [ 41 ]. Evapotranspiration was elevated during the summer months because of the high temperatures, and maximum sunshine hours and decreased during the winter due to lower temperatures. The impact of temperature on evapotranspiration can be described as directly correlated with the intensity and duration of solar radiation. Additionally, it was observed that an increase in radiation values results in a rise in evapotranspiration, indicating a direct relationship. The same trend was observed in all selected six stations. Mahmood and Ahmed [ 42 ] also stated that the evaporation rate was low in December and January due to the combination of low temperatures and high humidity and vice versa. The results also showed, the maximum evapotranspiration observed in BWP and KNP and minimum recorded in MBD among selected stations mainly due to high temperature and other climatic variations. Fig. 5 Full size image The bar graph shows the reference evapotranspiration of Wheat crops in the study area 3.2 Irrigation water requirement and crop water requirement of wheat Irrigation water requirement (IWR) is the amount of irrigation water supplied to a field to guarantee that the crop fulfills its entire CWR while minimizing water losses during the irrigation process [ 43 ]. CWR refers to the amount of water lost through crop evapotranspiration and is equivalent to the amount of water lost due to plant evapotranspiration. The total CWR, effective rainfall, and IWR for wheat crops across various agro-climatic zones were determined using the CROPWAT model based on the specific data from the study area. However, the findings of the present study cannot be compared fully due to the unavailability of similar studies in the same region. Tables 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 , 6 and 7 represent the crop water requirement of wheat in each district of Punjab using the CROPWAT model. The total irrigation water requirement of wheat in MBD, FSD, TTS, BWN, BWP, and KNP is 44.7, 102.2, 103.1, 119.1, 179, and 184.2 mm, respectively. From this data, it is clear that the irrigation requirement of the crop is higher in dry areas than in rainy regions. This study reflects that the crop grown in dry areas requires more water because of lesser rainfall than in cold climate areas with high rainfall. The FAO report [ 44 ] also describes that the area that receives more rainfall requires less irrigation than those areas where rainfall is minimal. Depending on the soil data, climate type, and cultivation practices, that crop requires different amounts of water to grow [ 45 ]. This study shows that the wheat crop requires more water during the growing stage than the other three stages. sections.text: The information provided to the CROPWAT for the selected study area encompassed details such as the crop variety, climatic station, soil type, and planting date. By entering these details into the software, it forecasts the evapotranspiration, total irrigation needs, and effective rainfall for the wheat crop. The reference evapotranspiration of wheat crops in the selected stations is shown in Fig. 5 . The results illustrate that evapotranspiration was highest in June and lowest in January. If the evapotranspiration value is high, it indicates increased evaporation, leading to a higher CWR. In contrast, if evapotranspiration is low, the crop’s water needs will be reduced [ 41 ]. Evapotranspiration was elevated during the summer months because of the high temperatures, and maximum sunshine hours and decreased during the winter due to lower temperatures. The impact of temperature on evapotranspiration can be described as directly correlated with the intensity and duration of solar radiation. Additionally, it was observed that an increase in radiation values results in a rise in evapotranspiration, indicating a direct relationship. The same trend was observed in all selected six stations. Mahmood and Ahmed [ 42 ] also stated that the evaporation rate was low in December and January due to the combination of low temperatures and high humidity and vice versa. sections.heading: 3.2 Irrigation water requirement and crop water requirement of wheat
sections.text: Irrigation water requirement (IWR) is the amount of irrigation water supplied to a field to guarantee that the crop fulfills its entire CWR while minimizing water losses during the irrigation process [ 43 ]. CWR refers to the amount of water lost through crop evapotranspiration and is equivalent to the amount of water lost due to plant evapotranspiration. The total CWR, effective rainfall, and IWR for wheat crops across various agro-climatic zones were determined using the CROPWAT model based on the specific data from the study area. However, the findings of the present study cannot be compared fully due to the unavailability of similar studies in the same region. Tables 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 , 6 and 7 represent the crop water requirement of wheat in each district of Punjab using the CROPWAT model. The total irrigation water requirement of wheat in MBD, FSD, TTS, BWN, BWP, and KNP is 44.7, 102.2, 103.1, 119.1, 179, and 184.2 mm, respectively. From this data, it is clear that the irrigation requirement of the crop is higher in dry areas than in rainy regions. This study reflects that the crop grown in dry areas requires more water because of lesser rainfall than in cold climate areas with high rainfall. The FAO report [ 44 ] also describes that the area that receives more rainfall requires less irrigation than those areas where rainfall is minimal. Depending on the soil data, climate type, and cultivation practices, that crop requires different amounts of water to grow [ 45 ]. This study shows that the wheat crop requires more water during the growing stage than the other three stages. Table 2 Irrigation water requirement of Wheat in Mandibahauddin Full size table Table 3 Irrigation water requirement of wheat in Faisalabad Full size table Table 4 Irrigation water requirement of Wheat in Toba Tek Singh Full size table Table 5 Irrigation water requirement of wheat in Bahawalnagar Full size table Table 6 Irrigation water requirement of Wheat in Bahawalpur Full size table Table 7 Irrigation water requirement of Wheat in Khanpur Full size table The districts where effective rain is not enough to fulfill the water requirement of the crop such as BWN, BWP, and KNP might be due to increasing temperature which ultimately increases the evapotranspiration here, the sprinkler system of irrigation is more beneficial. Another contributing factor to high evapotranspiration is rain, this region receives less rainfall as compared to another region of the study area. For sustainable agriculture, precipitation and irrigation are key water sources. The quantity and seasonal distribution of precipitation directly influence the water balance and irrigation needs of various crops [ 46 ]. The irrigation water requirement of each district can be better understood by Fig. 6 as when effective rainfall is low and crop water requirement is high the irrigation water requirement for that area increases, and when the effective rain is high covering the crop water requirement then there is less need of irrigation water respectively in every month. Fig. 6 Full size image The Irrigation water requirement concerning crop water requirement and effective rain of wheat in a Mandibahauddin, b Faisalabad, c Toba Tek Sing, d Bahawalnagar, e Bahawalpur, f Khanpur

sections.heading: 3.3 Irrigation scheduling of the selected stations
sections.text: Irrigation scheduling determines the appropriate amount of water to apply to crops and the timing of its delivery. The crop undergoes several initial, developmental, middle, and late stages, each with distinct irrigation needs. Therefore, effective planning is essential to ensure optimal water use throughout the crop’s growth cycle. The CROPWAT analysis shows that the gross irrigation requirement of MBD is 59.8 and net irrigation is 41.9 mm less than CWR which is 180 mm because this region gains enough rain, 132 mm of water requirement is fulfilled by effective rain therefore, the crop requires only one-time irrigation. FSD total gross irrigation is 113.8, net irrigation is 79.9, TTS and BWN gross irrigation is 115.7, and net irrigation is 81.0, BWP total gross irrigation is 175.6 mm and net is 122.9 mm, KNP gross irrigation is 235.4 mm and net irrigation is 164.8 mm shown in Figs. 7 , 8 , 9 , 10 , 11 and 12 . The successful irrigation practices to local conditions applied in [ 47 ], significantly enhance crop productivity and water efficiency. The total available moisture (TAM) in the figures shows all the water available to the crops. Plants take water from the root zones. As with time, water depletion reaches a level where irrigation requirements become high. If water is not applied at this stage, it may lead to crop wilting and destruction or a lower production rate. Fig. 7 Full size image Irrigation scheduling of wheat in Mandibahauddin Fig. 8 Full size image Irrigation scheduling of wheat in Faisalabad Fig. 9 Full size image Irrigation scheduling of wheat in Toba Tek Singh Fig. 10 Full size image Irrigation scheduling of wheat in Bahawalnagar Fig. 11 Full size image Irrigation scheduling of wheat in Bahawalpur Fig. 12 Full size image Irrigation scheduling of wheat in Khanpur

sections.heading: 4 Conclusion
sections.text: In this study, the IWR for the wheat crop was determined using the CROPWAT 8.0 software in six districts of Punjab showing as a representative of different agro-climatic outlooks developed by FAO. According to our findings, the highest IWR was observed in KNP 184.2 mm, as this area has a sub-tropical desert climate, less rainfall, and hot summers resulting in high water requirements and the lowest IWR was observed in MBD 44.7 mm. From the above discussion, we conclude that the climatic variations including rainfall, minimum and maximum temperature, humidity, sunshine, and wind speed occurred in all six stations of Punjab. The highest precipitation was observed in July and the overall trend was higher in central Punjab in MBD, TTS, and FSD than in southern Punjab including KNP, BWP, and BWN. The results of this study greatly develop our knowledge of the water requirements of wheat crops and help us understand better management of water resources and increased crop yields through cutting-edge technologies. The study outcomes will serve as guidance for the farmers in determining the appropriate time for irrigation for the wheat crop and assist with water resource planning in the future. Ultimately helping in the conservation of water. The present study is based on the past 10 years of climatic data for higher accuracy, so an identical study can be done on different crops in these districts.

sections.heading: Data availability
sections.text: The climatic data used in this study were provided by the Pakistan Meteorological Department (PMD) NAMC, Islamabad, Pakistan. For analysis in CROPWAT. The data are not publicly available but can be obtained from the PMD upon reasonable request and with permission.

sections.heading: Code availability
sections.text: No codes were used during the study.

sections.heading: Keywords
sections.text: CROPWAT Wheat Crop water requirement Irrigation scheduling Agro-climatic zones Punjab

paragraphs.text: You have full access to this open access article

Water scarcity, intensified by climate change and mismanagement, poses significant challenges to food production, particularly in arid and semi-arid regions like Punjab, Pakistan. This study successfully determined the crop water requirements (CWR) and irrigation scheduling for wheat across six agro-climatic zones in Punjab using CROPWAT software, highlighting the varying irrigation needs based on climatic conditions and providing essential guidance for effective water resource management.

Water scarcity is a global concern exacerbated by human activities, including water mismanagement. The water shortage intensely impacts food production, as inadequate water is applied to the field for irrigation. Proper irrigation systems help to boost crop productivity and ensure food security. In arid and semi-arid regions, a limited amount of rainfall demands an understanding of crop water requirements (CWR). Climate change is the major suspect that further complicates irrigation management because as the temperature rises, evapotranspiration increases, leading to greater water demand by crops. Changes in rainfall patterns contribute to drought or floods that adversely affect crop production. To address these challenges, irrigation scheduling is essential to mitigate the effect of climate change on crop yield. This study determines the CWR and irrigation scheduling of Wheat in six districts of Punjab across the agro-climatic zones defined by the Food and Agricultural Organization (FAO), using CROPWAT software. Evapotranspiration was calculated using the Penman–Monteith method that utilize the climate data from 2013 to 2023. The selected station includes Mandibahauddin (MBD), Faisalabad (FSD), Toba Tek Singh (TTS), Bahawalpur (BWP), Bahawalnagar (BWN), and Khanpur (KNP). The results indicate that CWR varies in different stages of the crop; the lowest water requirement is in the initial stage, and the highest water requirement is in the mid-stage during crop development. During the dry season, the water requirement is high because rain can’t fulfill the crop’s water needs. Notably, southern Punjab BWP and KNP show maximum irrigation water requirements of 179, and 184.2 mm due to the dry climate compared to central region MBD, highlighting the influence of climatic variations. Unlike the previous studies focusing on specific districts, this research paper provides comprehensive irrigation scheduling across Punjab’s agro-climatic zones. Future studies should expand to include other crops to refine irrigation practices further.

The scarcity of water and the degradation of water quality are being globally intensified by climate change. In Pakistan, due to significant susceptibility to climate change, agriculture frequently experiences negative consequences such as severe storms, floods, and droughts. [ 1 ]. Pakistan ranks 5th globally in the frequency of extreme weather events as per the Global Climate Risk Index [ 2 ]. In Pakistan, natural resources, particularly water, soil, and plants, are key for the survival of animals and humans but are continuously affected by climate change. Water is considered the quintessential substance among all elements, unparalleled in its remarkable properties, and is a crucial part of human well-being and socio-economic development. Water is the most important substance on Earth with approximately 0.3% of the global water resources available [ 3 , 4 ]. Surface water supplies are also shrinking due to industrialization and burdening the groundwater resources, and approximately 60 billion cubic meters (Bm3) of groundwater are annually pumped out [ 5 , 6 ].

When considering the water requirement in Pakistan, the agricultural sector is one of the most dominant water consumers and utilizes 70% of the world’s accessible water, with approximately 60% squandered [ 7 , 8 ]. In the area of 796,096 square kilometers [ 9 ], the arid regions make up two-thirds of Pakistan’s land area and have shallow water tables, the elevated air temperatures may cause faster evaporation, which would increase the aridity of the soil [ 10 ]. The nation’s problems with water scarcity could worsen if evapotranspiration rates keep rising and reservoirs start depleting quickly. Pakistan lies in an arid climate and relies heavily on irrigation due to insufficient precipitation on its agricultural lands and irrigation supports approximately, 18 million hectares of cultivable land [ 11 ]. Pakistan’s irrigation system ranks among the world’s largest sources of irrigation [ 12 ]. The total water resources in Pakistan is 180 bcm (billion cubic meters) from the Indus River system whereas, rainfall is the second, and groundwater is the third source with annual contributions of approximately 50 bcm and 50–60 bcm respectively [ 13 ]. In Pakistan, surface water is vital for agricultural activities. Approximately 95% of freshwater is used for agriculture and only 5% is allocated for domestic use [ 14 ]. Moreover, agriculture constitutes the primary source of income for 72% of Pakistan’s population. Crops including Wheat, Sugarcane, rice, cotton, and bananas are essential for the nation’s sustainable food demands and significantly boost its economic growth. Wheat the most important staple food of Pakistan plays a crucial role in the country’s agriculture. Punjab soil is favorable for wheat production as its cultivation surpassed 500,000 acres in the province in 2023. Pakistan’s economy hinges primarily on agriculture, with 70% of its population depending on this sector. Shifts in rainfall patterns and rising temperatures directly affect water resources and agriculture. Each year, escalating temperatures intensify the need for crop irrigation due to heightened evapotranspiration rates, demanding more water to sustain crop vitality [ 15 ]. So, reducing consumption and improving resource management is crucial.

Punjab has the largest area of irrigation 97% where maximum irrigation is provided to wheat, which is 60% [ 16 ] which contributes to 75% of the region's wheat production [ 9 ]. The state of Punjab in Pakistan is facing water scarcity due to the fast depletion of groundwater extracted for agricultural needs, especially in central Punjab because of the adverse effects of climate change and inefficient water use [ 17 ]. Punjab is a primary consumer of water for irrigation, so the irrigation system needs to be managed properly by assessing the water requirement system. To meet the irrigation demands, it is necessary to understand the CWR and the irrigation scheduling [ 18 ]. Accurately managing the CWR is key for agricultural systems [ 19 ]. Punjab is referred to as “the land of five rivers” [ 20 ], For many of the farmlands in Punjab, water is insufficient for crops and does not arrive in time [ 21 ] because of inadequate leveling and poor management water is not delivered properly to the irrigated area of Punjab [ 22 ]. Even farmers often over-irrigate their fields due to a lack of knowledge about CWR, believing that more water will lead to higher yields. Implementing a suitable irrigation strategy can mitigate the negative impacts of over-irrigation and help balance crop water needs with available water resources. The key to enhancing agriculture is implementing modern, effective irrigation strategies and improving water management practices with multiple software [ 23 ]. Engaging farmers with local agricultural extension is crucial in helping them manage irrigation scheduling effectively. These services train them through real-time experience through training or workshops. Additionally, these services will guide the farmer in using the best practices to control excess water use [ 24 ].

There are various models, such as Aqua Crop, CROPWAT, APSIM, etc., and every model has specific conditions as compared to other models, CROPWAT is much easier to use because it needs less data input than other software [ 25 ]. The CROPWAT model is used in this study to provide irrigation scheduling of Punjab. It calculates the irrigation water needs [ 26 ]. The previous literature doesn’t focus on crop water needs concerning climate in a province. Thus In the current study, using the CROPWAT tool, we analyzed the CWR, evapotranspiration, and irrigation scheduling of the wheat crop in Agro-climatic Zones of Punjab developed by FAO. Unlike the other general irrigation recommendations, this study uses unique climatic data, soil, and crop data for each FAO-defined agro-climatic zone in Punjab. This study has been conducted by the Pakistan Metrological Department, a national agromet center having expertise in agro-environmental studies.

CROPWAT is a window-based model developed by the FAO for calculating evapotranspiration and optimizing irrigation scheduling and design. This application uses the FAO’s Penman–Monteith Equation to determine evapotranspiration [ 12 ]. The key factors influencing the potential evapotranspiration value include temperature, wind speed, solar radiation, ambient temperature, and air humidity in the Monteith-Penman equation. With CROPWAT 8.0 software, we can determine the crop’s water requirements throughout the growing season and manage irrigation using rainwater effectively in the field [ 27 ]. The current scheduling techniques employed by farmers are outdated and ineffective. It is time to modernize these practices by utilizing CROPWAT to asses CWR, organize relevant data, model irrigation needs based on climate, crop, and soil information, and implement efficient scheduling [ 8 ]. The crop coefficient performs the irrigation schedule as it allocates the required amount of water to the crop. However, metrological data such as wind speed, temperature, and harvesting date greatly affect the Kc value. Therefore, it is important to make corrections to the data.

Punjab is the second-largest and most populated province, accounting for 25.9% of the country’s total land area, which spans 20.63 million hectares, out of this 72% is suitable for cropping [ 28 ]. Punjab’s total cropped area accounts for 16.68 million hectares. In 2023–24, the net sown area of wheat covered 9.6 million hectares, accounting for 53% of the total [ 29 ], giving 28,175.5 tons of wheat production [ 30 ]. This study was conducted in the seven Agro-climatic zones of Punjab classified by FAO based on the Thornthwaite Moisture Index (TMI). MBD FSD, TTS, BWN, BWP, and KNP are the representatives of each zone, as shown in Fig. 1 . None of the stations was selected from Zone 1 because this is a rain-fed zone as wheat in this zone does not require irrigation and the water requirement of the crop is fulfilled by rainwater. The complete description of the area, climate, and annual rainfall of these districts is mentioned in Table 1 .
The figure shows the selected districts from each Agro-climatic zone of Punjab that were used for the crop water requirement and irrigation scheduling of wheat

CROPWAT model required mainly three types of data; climatic, soil, and crop data. In the current study, we used the climatic data obtained from the Pakistan Metrological Department (PMD). Climatic data includes five parameters; minimum and maximum temperature, rainfall, sunshine hour, wind speed, relative humidity, and physical data such as latitude, longitude, and altitude. These datasets were utilized to calculate the CWR. The lowest minimum average temperature observed at the selected station was recorded in January and the highest maximum average temperature was recorded in May and June, standard deviation is applied to see the data consistency as shown in Fig. 2 .
Monthly mean of Minimum and Maximum temperature of the selected station from 2013 to 2023 a Mandibahauddin, b Faisalabad, c T.T Singh, d Bahawalnagar, e Bahawalpur, f Khanpur

The precipitation pattern of the study area shows maximum rainfall in the monsoon season from July to September whereas, minimum rain was recorded from December to February under the influence of western disturbance is exhibited in Fig. 3 . The parameters of humidity, wind speed, and sunshine hours were also considered as they are the major factors for evapotranspiration. January is the most humid month in the study area. The maximum sunshine hours from the selected six stations are observed in May. The data of average humidity, sunshine hours, and wind speed of the study area are displayed in Fig. 4 .
The graph shows the monthly mean rainfall in the study area from 2013 to 2023
The web graphs show the Humidity, sunshine hour, and wind speed in the study area of a Mandibahauddin, b Faisalabad, c T.T Singh, d Bahawalnagar, e Bahawalpur, f Khanpur

Wheat is the major crop of Punjab and occupies the largest portion of cropped land. In 2015, the wheat crop was planted in 42% of the total crop area [ 37 ]. To compute the Crop Irrigation Requirement, CROPWAT requires several crop parameters, including planting and harvesting dates, number of days at different stages of growth, rooting depth, allowable depletion, crop coefficient (Kc), and yield response. The date of planting and harvesting of crops is selected after a thorough review of different websites and sources, and the remaining factors were taken from FAO software CROPWAT.

The soil type of the study area ranges from sandy loam to loam. The CROPWAT software requires fundamental soil data, including maximum rainfall infiltration rate, total soil moisture available, maximum root depth, Soil moisture depletion, and initial soil moisture content. The software used for the current study includes information on soil data. The required soil type and data are imported from the software.

Evapotranspiration refers to the combined loss of water through transpiration from plants and evaporation from the soil surface. The FAO Penman–Monteith equation is used for the calculation of evapotranspiration by the CROPWAT model as shown in Eq. 1 [ 19 ].
Pa = average air density at constant pressure.
es—ea = vapor pressure deficiency of the soil.
ETo is reference evapotranspiration measured in millimeters per day. ETo can be represented by the following Eq. 2
u2 = the speed of wind at a height of 2 m (ms −1 ).

Effective rainfall is the portion of rainfall that is retained in the soil profile and contributes to crop growth. The effective rainfall in the CROPWAT model was calculated using the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Soil Conservation Service (SCS) formula shown in Eqs. 3 and 4 [ 38 ].
CWR is defined as the quantity of water needed to meet evapotranspiration demands for optimal crop growth, and it is calculated by using Eq. 5 [ 39 ]

The irrigation water requirement is the quantity of water required to meet CWR after accounting for any effective rainfall. This irrigation water requirement is for disease-free crops growing in extensive fields with non-restrictive water and soil conditions and sufficient fertility. The irrigation water requirement is greater than the CWR when agriculture relies solely on irrigation. However, when both irrigation and rainfall are used, the irrigation water requirement is slightly lower than the CWR due to the supplementary water provided by rainfall. It can be calculated using the Eq. 6 . The net irrigation requirement of a crop is calculated by finding the difference between effective rain and crop evapotranspiration as given in Eq. ( 7 ) [ 40 ].

The information provided to the CROPWAT for the selected study area encompassed details such as the crop variety, climatic station, soil type, and planting date. By entering these details into the software, it forecasts the evapotranspiration, total irrigation needs, and effective rainfall for the wheat crop.

The reference evapotranspiration of wheat crops in the selected stations is shown in Fig. 5 . The results illustrate that evapotranspiration was highest in June and lowest in January. If the evapotranspiration value is high, it indicates increased evaporation, leading to a higher CWR. In contrast, if evapotranspiration is low, the crop’s water needs will be reduced [ 41 ]. Evapotranspiration was elevated during the summer months because of the high temperatures, and maximum sunshine hours and decreased during the winter due to lower temperatures. The impact of temperature on evapotranspiration can be described as directly correlated with the intensity and duration of solar radiation. Additionally, it was observed that an increase in radiation values results in a rise in evapotranspiration, indicating a direct relationship. The same trend was observed in all selected six stations. Mahmood and Ahmed [ 42 ] also stated that the evaporation rate was low in December and January due to the combination of low temperatures and high humidity and vice versa. The results also showed, the maximum evapotranspiration observed in BWP and KNP and minimum recorded in MBD among selected stations mainly due to high temperature and other climatic variations.
The bar graph shows the reference evapotranspiration of Wheat crops in the study area

Irrigation water requirement (IWR) is the amount of irrigation water supplied to a field to guarantee that the crop fulfills its entire CWR while minimizing water losses during the irrigation process [ 43 ]. CWR refers to the amount of water lost through crop evapotranspiration and is equivalent to the amount of water lost due to plant evapotranspiration. The total CWR, effective rainfall, and IWR for wheat crops across various agro-climatic zones were determined using the CROPWAT model based on the specific data from the study area. However, the findings of the present study cannot be compared fully due to the unavailability of similar studies in the same region.

Tables 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 , 6 and 7 represent the crop water requirement of wheat in each district of Punjab using the CROPWAT model. The total irrigation water requirement of wheat in MBD, FSD, TTS, BWN, BWP, and KNP is 44.7, 102.2, 103.1, 119.1, 179, and 184.2 mm, respectively. From this data, it is clear that the irrigation requirement of the crop is higher in dry areas than in rainy regions. This study reflects that the crop grown in dry areas requires more water because of lesser rainfall than in cold climate areas with high rainfall. The FAO report [ 44 ] also describes that the area that receives more rainfall requires less irrigation than those areas where rainfall is minimal. Depending on the soil data, climate type, and cultivation practices, that crop requires different amounts of water to grow [ 45 ]. This study shows that the wheat crop requires more water during the growing stage than the other three stages.

The districts where effective rain is not enough to fulfill the water requirement of the crop such as BWN, BWP, and KNP might be due to increasing temperature which ultimately increases the evapotranspiration here, the sprinkler system of irrigation is more beneficial. Another contributing factor to high evapotranspiration is rain, this region receives less rainfall as compared to another region of the study area. For sustainable agriculture, precipitation and irrigation are key water sources. The quantity and seasonal distribution of precipitation directly influence the water balance and irrigation needs of various crops [ 46 ]. The irrigation water requirement of each district can be better understood by Fig. 6 as when effective rainfall is low and crop water requirement is high the irrigation water requirement for that area increases, and when the effective rain is high covering the crop water requirement then there is less need of irrigation water respectively in every month.
The Irrigation water requirement concerning crop water requirement and effective rain of wheat in a Mandibahauddin, b Faisalabad, c Toba Tek Sing, d Bahawalnagar, e Bahawalpur, f Khanpur

Irrigation scheduling determines the appropriate amount of water to apply to crops and the timing of its delivery. The crop undergoes several initial, developmental, middle, and late stages, each with distinct irrigation needs. Therefore, effective planning is essential to ensure optimal water use throughout the crop’s growth cycle. The CROPWAT analysis shows that the gross irrigation requirement of MBD is 59.8 and net irrigation is 41.9 mm less than CWR which is 180 mm because this region gains enough rain, 132 mm of water requirement is fulfilled by effective rain therefore, the crop requires only one-time irrigation. FSD total gross irrigation is 113.8, net irrigation is 79.9, TTS and BWN gross irrigation is 115.7, and net irrigation is 81.0, BWP total gross irrigation is 175.6 mm and net is 122.9 mm, KNP gross irrigation is 235.4 mm and net irrigation is 164.8 mm shown in Figs. 7 , 8 , 9 , 10 , 11 and 12 . The successful irrigation practices to local conditions applied in [ 47 ], significantly enhance crop productivity and water efficiency. The total available moisture (TAM) in the figures shows all the water available to the crops. Plants take water from the root zones. As with time, water depletion reaches a level where irrigation requirements become high. If water is not applied at this stage, it may lead to crop wilting and destruction or a lower production rate.
Irrigation scheduling of wheat in Mandibahauddin
Irrigation scheduling of wheat in Faisalabad
Irrigation scheduling of wheat in Toba Tek Singh
Irrigation scheduling of wheat in Bahawalnagar
Irrigation scheduling of wheat in Bahawalpur
Irrigation scheduling of wheat in Khanpur

In this study, the IWR for the wheat crop was determined using the CROPWAT 8.0 software in six districts of Punjab showing as a representative of different agro-climatic outlooks developed by FAO. According to our findings, the highest IWR was observed in KNP 184.2 mm, as this area has a sub-tropical desert climate, less rainfall, and hot summers resulting in high water requirements and the lowest IWR was observed in MBD 44.7 mm. From the above discussion, we conclude that the climatic variations including rainfall, minimum and maximum temperature, humidity, sunshine, and wind speed occurred in all six stations of Punjab. The highest precipitation was observed in July and the overall trend was higher in central Punjab in MBD, TTS, and FSD than in southern Punjab including KNP, BWP, and BWN. The results of this study greatly develop our knowledge of the water requirements of wheat crops and help us understand better management of water resources and increased crop yields through cutting-edge technologies. The study outcomes will serve as guidance for the farmers in determining the appropriate time for irrigation for the wheat crop and assist with water resource planning in the future. Ultimately helping in the conservation of water. The present study is based on the past 10 years of climatic data for higher accuracy, so an identical study can be done on different crops in these districts.

The climatic data used in this study were provided by the Pakistan Meteorological Department (PMD) NAMC, Islamabad, Pakistan. For analysis in CROPWAT. The data are not publicly available but can be obtained from the PMD upon reasonable request and with permission.
